Description: Any `thread::sleep(..)` or `sleep(Duration::..)` in TEST code. A wall-clock sleep as a stop condition is a race against the scheduler: under load the machine loses and the test fails with nothing actually broken, which blocks unrelated PRs and trains people to bypass the gate. `fs_concurrent_put_get_interleaved` did exactly that — its threads looped `while !stop` while the main thread slept 50ms then set `stop`, so a thread not scheduled inside that window did ZERO iterations and failed its own `> 0` sanity assertion. It flaked CI on a licensing PR that touched no code at all. Projection: `smell-projector` scans the INVERSE of `unsafe_sites`/`unwrap_sites` — everything under `/tests/` and `/benches/`, plus the `#[cfg(test)]` tail of `/src/` files — since those rules audit production code and this one audits the tests. Multi-line raw strings and comment lines are excluded. BASELINED, not gated at zero: the remaining occurrences are not one fix. Bounded-loop sleeps become fixed iteration counts (mechanical); daemon/blackbox ones are waiting for a process to become ready and need a real poll-until-condition predicate with a ceiling that only fails on genuine hang — deleting those without the predicate makes them worse. Bead `ley-line-open-c6101e` tracks the burn-down; this rule holds the line so no NEW sleeps land meanwhile.
Query: SELECT s.source_id, s.node_id, s.start_row, s.start_col, s.end_row, s.end_col, 1 AS metric FROM sleep_sites s WHERE 1=1 %s ORDER BY s.source_id, s.start_row
